preFix.title: Frame loom continuous warp technique
preFix.subtitle: How to wind a continuous warp on a frame loom for even tension and consistent thread spacing
preFix.excerpt: A continuous warp is wound from a single length of yarn looped around the pegs or bars of a frame loom rather than cut and tied as individual threads. This method produces even tension across all warp threads and is faster to set up than attaching individual ends. A shed stick placed during warping holds one shed open for weaving.
preFix.sourceNotes: Continuous warp frame loom technique is consistent across beginner weaving instruction published by UK and US guild resources. Observation-led.

content.text: on a frame loom is wound by looping a single length of yarn around the top and bottom bars in sequence without cutting between threads. The loops at the top and bottom create the return paths for the warp; a
slipped through alternate threads during warping holds the first shed ready for weaving. Understanding the
before you begin allows you to plan the number of threads needed for the weaving width.

items.name: Frame loom with pegs, notches, or smooth bars at top and bottom
Smooth, strong warp yarn: cotton, linen, or fine wool
Flat shed stick or ruler, at least as long as the loom is wide

content.text: Tie the yarn end to the bottom-left peg or bar with a firm knot. Bring the yarn straight up and over the top-left peg, then down and around the second peg from the left at the bottom, then up and over the second peg at the top. Continue in this figure-of-eight pattern across the full width. The yarn should make a complete loop over each top peg and under each bottom peg in sequence. Keep the tension consistent on every pass: each thread should feel equally taut when pressed lightly with a finger. When the desired width is reached, tie off with a knot at the last peg.

Once the warp is fully wound, slide the shed stick through the warp from one side, going under the first thread, over the second, under the third, and so on across the full width. When the shed stick is on its edge, it separates alternate threads and opens the first weaving shed. Leave the shed stick in place at the top of the warp while weaving and slide it toward you each time you need to open the first shed. The second shed is created by picking up the opposite alternate threads with a finger or a second stick before each return pass.

items.fix: Unwind and re-warp, keeping the same light, even tension on every pass. If the pegs are uneven in height, place a piece of card under the lower ones before re-warping.
items.cause: The yarn was wound with inconsistent pull, or the pegs are at different heights
items.problem: The warp threads are uneven in tension with some loose and some tight

items.fix: Gently ease each alternate thread over the shed stick rather than sliding. For future warping, spread the warp threads more widely or use a finer warp yarn so the shed opens more cleanly.
items.cause: The warp spacing is too close for the yarn weight, making the shed narrow
items.problem: The shed stick will not slide across the warp without catching
